title: Heatwave
body: Extreme heat is a universal health risk. Use these accessible, low-cost strategies to regulate your body temperature and stay safe.
tips: Drink tap water regularly; do not wait for thirst, as hydration is the primary defense against heat stress.
Seek shade or public cooling spaces like libraries or religious centers if your home becomes dangerously hot.
Use wet cloths or cool water on your skin (wrists, neck, and armpits) to lower your core body temperature quickly.
Wear any loose-fitting, light-colored clothing you own to allow air to circulate and sweat to evaporate.
Watch for red flag symptoms like confusion or stopping sweating, which indicate a medical emergency.

title: Cold / Freeze risk
body: Prolonged exposure to extreme cold can lead to hypothermia or frostbite. Protect your body heat and secure your home environment.
tips: Wear multiple layers of loose-fitting clothing to trap body heat rather than one single heavy layer.
Cover all exposed skin, especially extremities like fingers, toes, and ears, which are most prone to frostbite.
Keep moving to generate body heat, but avoid overexertion that leads to sweating, as wet skin cools faster.
Block drafts around doors and windows using towels or blankets to keep heat inside living areas.
Eat warm, high-energy meals and drink warm fluids to provide your body with the fuel needed to produce heat.

title: Storm / High wind
body: Strong winds can turn everyday objects into dangerous projectiles and cause structural damage. Prioritize physical cover.
tips: Stay indoors and away from windows, glass doors, and skylights that could shatter from flying debris.
Secure or bring inside loose outdoor items like trash cans, garden tools, and furniture before the wind peaks.
Avoid standing near trees, tall poles, or power lines, as these are the most likely to fall during high gusts.
If caught outside, seek a low-lying area or a sturdy building and protect your head with your arms.
Listen to local authorities for updates, as high winds often precede more severe weather or power outages.
imgAlt: an image represents storm / high wind

title: Heavy rain / flood risk
body: Flash floods can occur within minutes. Never underestimate the power of moving water, even if it appears shallow.
tips: Never walk, swim, or drive through floodwaters; just six inches of moving water can knock you off your feet.
Move to higher ground immediately if you live in a low-lying area or near a body of water.
Disconnect electrical appliances to prevent electric shock if water begins to enter your home.
Avoid contact with floodwater, as it is often contaminated with sewage, chemicals, or hidden sharp objects.
Keep an emergency kit with clean bottled water and a flashlight in an easy-to-reach, high location.
imgAlt: an image represents heavy rain / flood risk

title: Thunderstorm
body: Lightning is a major threat during thunderstorms. Remember the rule: When thunder roars, go indoors.
tips: Move to a substantial, fully enclosed building or a metal-topped vehicle immediately upon hearing thunder.
Avoid using corded phones or touching electrical appliances, as lightning can travel through wiring.
Stay away from plumbing, including sinks, baths, and showers, as lightning can travel through metal pipes.
If caught outdoors with no shelter, avoid tall isolated objects like trees and stay away from open water.
Wait at least 30 minutes after the last clap of thunder before leaving your shelter to ensure the threat has passed.

title: Fog risk
body: Fog significantly reduces visibility and can make navigation dangerous. Use extra caution and rely on specialized lighting.
tips: Use low-beam headlights only; high beams reflect off the moisture and actually make it harder to see.
Reduce your speed immediately and increase the following distance between you and other travelers.
Use pavement markings (the side lines of the road) as a guide to stay in your lane rather than center lights.
Minimize distractions inside your vehicle and open your window slightly to listen for traffic you cannot see.
If visibility becomes near zero, pull over to a safe area away from the road and turn off all lights except hazard flashers.

title: Snow
body: Heavy snow can lead to isolation and physical strain. Prioritize warmth and safe movement to avoid injuries.
tips: Walk like a penguin on icy surfaces: keep your center of gravity over your front leg to avoid slipping.
Shovel snow in small amounts and lift with your legs, not your back, to prevent heart strain and muscle injury.
Keep exhaust vents for furnaces or water heaters clear of snow buildup to prevent carbon monoxide poisoning.
Ensure you have a basic emergency kit with food, blankets, and a flashlight in case of power or travel disruptions.
Apply sand or salt to walkways to improve traction, but avoid touching chemicals with bare skin.

title: Earthquake
body: Earthquakes happen without warning. Your immediate reaction determines your safety during the shaking.
tips: Drop, Cover, and Hold On: get under a sturdy piece of furniture and protect your head and neck.
Stay away from glass, windows, outside doors and walls, and anything that could fall, such as lighting fixtures.
If you are outdoors, move to an open area away from buildings, streetlights, and utility wires.
If you are in a vehicle, stop as quickly as safety permits and stay inside until the shaking stops.
Expect aftershocks; these are often less intense than the main quake but can still cause weakened structures to fall.
